aliases: Yìn Dù Pào Mǐ Shā Lā

romanized: Yìn Dù Pào Mǐ Shā Lā

food_subgroup: Mixed puffed rice chaat with fried sev noodles, chopped vegetables, and sweet-sour chutneys

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Bhel Puri or Indian chaat preparations. No defensible exact or close FoodOn URI could be identified without fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Snacks, savoury — closest parent category in FoodEx2 for mixed savoury puffed grain snack preparations. No specific Bhel Puri code exists; this is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bhel Puri. A0GXM (savoury snacks) is used as the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for Bhel Puri or equivalent Indian chaat preparations.

allergens: gluten (sev contains gram flour; papdi contains wheat flour)
possible groundnut traces in some preparations

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bhel Puri is approximately 150 grams, equivalent to one plate as typically served at an Indian chaat stall or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Bhel Puri / Puffed Rice Chaat composite
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for puffed rice (murmura), sev, and component vegetables. Bhel Puri composite values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data weighted by standard recipe proportions (puffed rice ~40%, sev ~15%, vegetables ~25%, chutneys ~20%). Values are consistent with the input record which was flagged as ICMR-NIN verified. Core macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for puffed rice (NDB 20051) and gram flour sev.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — puffed rice (murmura) as primary GI driver; Atkinson FS et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: Puffed rice (murmura) has a published GI of approximately 82–87 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Bhel Puri is dominated by puffed rice (~40% by weight). The composite GI of ~87 reflects the puffed rice base with minor moderating effects from fat (sev) and fibre (vegetables). GL per 150g serving = (87 × 40.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 35.2. This is a referenced analogous estimate; no direct Bhel Puri GI study was identified in the literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100g (450 mg per 150g serving) from chaat masala (black salt / kala namak) and tamarind chutney
hypertension.guidance: One 150g serving contributes approximately 450 mg sodium (~20% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive patients); individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced chaat masala.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~87) and very high glycemic load (~35.2 per 150g serving) driven by puffed rice (murmura), one of the highest-GI carbohydrate foods known
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhel Puri is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to puffed rice delivering a postprandial glucose spike equivalent to consuming white rice and a sweetened beverage simultaneously; if unavoidable at social events, consume only the vegetable components and minimise puffed rice and sev intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of antioxidant-rich vegetables and tamarind
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The lipid profile of Bhel Puri is favourable for cardiovascular health with very low saturated fat and no trans fat; however, the moderate sodium content (450 mg per serving) warrants caution for patients with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk, and the high glycemic load may contribute to postprandial triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat; fresh vegetables provide Vitamin C and polyphenols; tamarind provides tartaric acid and antioxidant polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bhel Puri is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its low saturated fat content and anti-inflammatory vegetable components, though the high-GI puffed rice base may contribute to systemic inflammation via postprandial glucose spikes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhel Puri is Mumbai's most iconic street snack and a quintessential Indian chaat preparation. It is assembled — not cooked — by combining puffed rice (murmura or kurmura), crispy fried gram flour noodles (sev), finely chopped raw onion, diced tomato, boiled potato cubes, fresh coriander leaves, and crushed papdi (crispy wheat flour discs). The dish is dressed with tangy tamarind chutney, spicy green chili chutney, a squeeze of fresh lemon juice, and a dusting of chaat masala spice blend. The result is a complex interplay of sweet, sour, spicy, and salty flavours with a satisfying crunch that softens rapidly after mixing — Bhel Puri must be eaten immediately after assembly to preserve its textural contrast. Nutritionally, one 150g serving provides approximately 210 kcal, 4.5g protein, 4.5g fat, and 40.5g carbohydrate. Despite its light, salad-like appearance, Bhel Puri is among the worst choices for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: puffed rice has one of the highest glycemic index values of any food (GI ~87), and a single serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 35 — equivalent to the combined glycemic impact of a bowl of white rice and a sweetened beverage. The air-expansion process that creates puffed rice completely destroys starch granule integrity, causing glucose absorption faster than white rice or glucose solution. Sodium content is moderate at 300 mg/100g (450 mg per serving), primarily from chaat masala and tamarind chutney. The dish is vegan, dairy-free, and halal. In Singapore, Bhel Puri is available at Indian fast food restaurants (Komala Vilas, Saravana Bhavan), Indian canteens, and hawker food courts. The clinical teaching point: 'light and crunchy does not mean low-glycemic.'

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, the primary approved source for Indian dishes, with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central component data. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for puffed rice as the dominant GI driver. Input record was flagged as ICMR-NIN verified with confidence 1.0 for all primary macros.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (naturally zero — this is a plant-based vegan preparation with no animal-derived ingredients). Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (naturally zero — no animal products present). Vitamin D is confirmed zero (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients). Trans fat is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN component analysis. GL per serving calculated as: GI (87) × available carbohydrate per serving (40.5g) / 100 = 35.2. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by ICMR-NIN for this composite preparation; set to -1. Water content not reported for composite preparation; set to -1. The input record contained duplicate portion entries; deduplicated to a single canonical 150g serving. The input cultural.region_tags and country_of_origin fields contained narrative text rather than structured values; corrected in this output. Ontology: FoodEx2 code A0GXM (savoury snacks) used as closest defensible parent; no specific Bhel Puri code exists in FoodEx2, FoodOn, or SNOMED CT. Q8 fix (iteration 3): all aliases_by_language entries — including Chinese variants (印度泡米沙拉), Tamil romanized (Pēl Pūri), Chinese romanized (Yìn Dù Pào Mǐ Shā Lā), and Malay variant (Bel Puri) — are now explicitly present in the flat aliases array, ensuring full consistency between aliases_by_language and aliases.
